name: Star Field
explanation: A multitap space reverb. A network of discrete, scattered early-reflection taps pings outward across the stereo field and then blossoms into a diffuse, gently modulated ambient tail — a constellation of reflections decaying into deep space rather than a smooth hall. Controls: Size, Taps/Spread, Decay, Modulation and dry/wet Mix.

name: Iron Drive
explanation: Transformer-style iron-core saturation. The iron saturates earliest in the lows, so a tunable low-end push is fed back into the drive stage, a hysteresis-flavoured soft saturator adds asymmetric even + odd harmonic warmth, and a gentle high-frequency rounding tames the top. A post Tone tilt, dry/wet Mix and Output trim finish it. Controls: Drive, Low, Tone, Mix and Output.

name: Vox Strings
explanation: A paraphonic string + voice ensemble. Every held note stacks three slightly detuned sawtooth strings against a breathy, formant-filtered choir 'aah' layer; the two blend, swell through a slow attack/release contour, and pour into a lush three-phase ensemble chorus that spreads the section across the stereo field. Controls: Strings Level, Voice Level, Ensemble, Attack, Release, Tone and Level.

name: Vector Wave
explanation: A polyphonic vector / wave-sequencing synthesizer. Four single-cycle waves are generated in code and placed on the corners of a square morph pad; a Vector X/Y joystick bilinearly crossfades between them while a slow Wave-Sequence rate walks that point around the pad so the timbre evolves over time on a held note. Up to eight voices keyed per note, each running two slightly detuned vector oscillators into a resonant low-pass with an attack/release contour. Controls: Vector X, Vector Y, Sequence Rate, Cutoff, Attack, Release, Detune and Level.

name: Discrete EQ
explanation: A punchy, forward discrete-electronics console equaliser. Three cascaded bands modelled on a vintage 500-series discrete-op-amp EQ: a low band that switches between musical shelf and bell, a sweepable mid bell with reciprocal proportional-Q (the bell sits wide at small boosts and narrows as you push it hard), and a high band that switches between shelf and bell. The whole strip is followed by a touch of discrete-stage drive for that forward console weight. Controls: Low Gain, Low Shape (shelf/bell), Mid Freq, Mid Gain, High Gain, High Shape (shelf/bell) and Drive.

name: Multiband
explanation: A three-band dynamics processor for mastering. The signal is split into low, mid and high bands by two crossovers (~220 Hz and ~2.5 kHz), and each band is compressed independently with its own threshold so the lows can pump without dulling the highs. A shared ratio sets how hard each band squeezes; the bands recombine through a makeup/output trim. Controls: Low Thresh, Mid Thresh, High Thresh, Ratio and Output.

name: Patch 2600
explanation: A monophonic semi-modular synthesizer in the spirit of the classic patchable bench instruments. Three oscillators (saw, pulse and triangle) are spread by a shared Detune and can be cross-multiplied by a switchable ring modulator for metallic clangor, then driven into an aggressive resonant low-pass. A dedicated filter ADSR (scaled by Filter Env Amount) sweeps the cutoff while a separate amplitude envelope shapes the level, with Glide for portamento and last-note priority. Controls: Detune, RingMod, Cutoff, Resonance, FilterEnvAmt, Attack, Release, Glide and Level.

name: Wave Table
explanation: A polyphonic wavetable synthesizer in the spirit of early-1980s German digital hardware. Eight single-cycle waves are generated in code, spanning a pure sine through ever richer harmonic spectra into bright, hollow formant shapes. A Wave Position control scans smoothly through the bank with linear interpolation between tables, morphing the timbre in real time. Each of up to eight voices runs a wavetable oscillator into a resonant 4-pole low-pass shaped by its own filter envelope and amount, then an amplitude envelope, so chords ring with independent contours. Controls: Wave Position, Cutoff, Resonance, Filter Env Amount, Attack, Release, Detune and Level.

name: Bus Glue
explanation: A VCA-style stereo bus compressor for the classic mix-glue character. A stereo-linked RMS detector feeds a dB-domain gain computer with a gentle soft knee and three switchable ratios (2:1 / 4:1 / 10:1). Stepped Attack and Release times — with a program-dependent auto-release at the top of the Release range — shape a smooth gain-reduction envelope, and a Makeup gain restores level. Loud, dense material is gently pulled together more than quiet passages, gluing a mix into a cohesive whole. Controls: Threshold, Ratio, Attack, Release and Makeup.

name: Studio Delay
explanation: A pristine rackmount digital delay. A fractionally-interpolated stereo delay line gives crystal-clear echoes that decay smoothly; feedback regenerates the repeats (bounded near maximum so it never runs away), an optional gentle modulation adds subtle movement, a high/low damping filter darkens or brightens each repeat, and a stereo width control offsets the left/right taps for a wide image. Controls: Time, Feedback, Modulation, Damping, Width and dry/wet Mix.

name: Phase Synth
explanation: A polyphonic phase-distortion digital synthesizer. Instead of analog oscillators and filters, each of its up to eight voices reads a sine table through a warped phase ramp: a non-linear phase map bends the read so a pure sine morphs toward saw, square and resonant timbres. The warp depth is driven both by a static Shape control and by a per-voice distortion (DCW) envelope, so notes ring bright on the attack and settle toward a sine as they decay. Controls: Shape (sine to bright), DCW Amount, DCW Decay, Attack, Release, Detune and Level.

name: Sonic Max
explanation: A psychoacoustic clarity and phase enhancer. The signal is split into low, mid and high bands; the lows are delayed a touch to re-align their timing against the highs, restoring transient punch. Process lifts high-frequency presence for air and definition, while Lo Contour blooms the low end for weight and body. The bands recombine through a soft output trim and dry/wet Mix, so the effect stays subtle and bounded. Controls: Process, Lo Contour, Output and Mix.

name: Robot Voice
explanation: A channel vocoder in the classic analog-bank style. Your input is the modulator: it is split into 8, 12 or 16 band-pass bands, each tracked by an envelope follower. An internal carrier — a buzzy sawtooth blended toward white noise — is filtered into the same bands and multiplied by those envelopes, so the carrier is forced to wear the spectral shape of your input. The result is the unmistakable talking, robotic timbre. Controls: Bands, Carrier (saw to noise), Formant band shift, Resonance and dry/wet Mix.

name: Duo Synth
explanation: A duophonic analog lead synthesizer. Two sawtooth oscillators independently track the two most recent held notes (lowest note -> OSC 1, newest -> OSC 2), so two keys sound as two distinct pitches; a single key locks both oscillators together. An optional hard-sync resets OSC 2 to OSC 1 for a buzzy, formant-rich tone, and the pair feeds a shared resonant 4-pole low-pass swept by one ADSR for a sharp, punchy character. Controls: Detune, Sync, Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Attack, Release and Level.

name: Cinematic Poly
explanation: A lush, grand polyphonic synthesizer for cinematic brass-leaning pads and swells. Up to eight voices keyed by note, each running two detuned layers of saw + pulse oscillators into their own resonant low-pass with a filter ADSR, then an amplitude ADSR; a gentle global ensemble chorus and brightness tilt add the wide, choral sheen. Controls: Detune, Cutoff, Resonance, Filter Env Amount, Attack, Release, Brightness and Level.

name: Whammy
explanation: An extreme expression-pedal pitch shifter. A dual-pointer, crossfaded delay-line shifter chases a target pitch ratio that the Pedal sweeps from dry (heel) to full shift (toe), so you can dive-bomb and soar in real time. Mode selects the direction — 0 up, 1 down, 2 a shimmering dual-octave stack — across a wide ±24-semitone Pitch range. Crossfaded read pointers keep big pitch leaps and dives click-free. Controls: Pitch, Pedal, Mode and dry/wet Mix.

name: Squelch Filter
explanation: An acid envelope filter: a resonant 4-pole transistor-ladder low-pass whose cutoff is opened by an envelope follower tracking the input dynamics, summed with a manual base cutoff. High resonance gives the signature squelch and can near self-oscillation while a saturated feedback path keeps it stable. On transients the filter snaps open and quacks shut. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Decay and dry/wet Mix.

name: Poly Moog
explanation: A fat, warm polyphonic analog synthesizer with up to eight independent voices. Each voice runs three detuned oscillators (two band-limited saws and a pulse) into a 4-pole ladder-style low-pass with tanh saturation, swept by its own filter envelope, then an amplitude envelope; chords ring as separate fat voices. Controls: Detune, Cutoff, Resonance, Filter Env Amount, Attack, Release and Level.

name: String Ensemble
explanation: A paraphonic 70s-style string machine. Every held note drives two band-limited sawtooth oscillators (the note plus its octave) summed into one bus, swelled by a shared slow Attack/Release envelope so chords bloom like a bowed string section. The bus runs through a triple-tap BBD-style ensemble chorus — three slow LFO-modulated delay lines — for the signature lush shimmering movement. Controls: Attack, Release, Ensemble, Tone and Level.

name: Freq Shifter
explanation: A Bode-style single-sideband frequency shifter. It splits the input into a 90-degree quadrature pair with a Hilbert-transform allpass network, modulates by a quadrature carrier, and keeps only one sideband — shifting every frequency by a fixed number of Hertz rather than by a ratio. A harmonic input becomes inharmonic and metallic. Controls: Shift (-500..500 Hz), Sideband (down/up), Feedback for shimmering inharmonic cascades, and dry/wet Mix.

name: Scream Filter
explanation: An aggressive Sallen-Key resonant filter. A high-pass and a low-pass run in series, each a 2-pole cell with a saturated resonance feedback path that can ring up to self-oscillation and scream. Signal is driven into that feedback with a tanh nonlinearity for the characteristic biting, distorted tone; the saturator also caps the self-oscillation so the output stays stable at extreme settings. Controls: LP Cutoff, HP Cutoff, Resonance, Drive and dry/wet Mix.

name: Vari-Mu
explanation: A program-dependent variable-mu tube compressor. As the input pushes harder above the threshold, the gain-reduction tube bends progressively more, so a loud signal is squeezed harder than a quiet one (a soft, rising-ratio knee with no fixed ratio). A slow, level-dependent attack feeds a dual time-constant release for a gentle, natural recovery, and subtle even-order valve harmonics add warmth. Controls: Input, Threshold, Time Constant and Output.

name: Pitch Shifter
explanation: A delay-line pitch shifter and harmonizer. A circular delay line is read by two crossfaded pointers that drift at a rate set by the pitch ratio, producing a click-free shifted voice that is regenerated through a feedback path and blended with the dry signal. Controls: Shift (semitones), Fine (cents), Feedback and dry/wet Mix.

name: Granular Cloud
explanation: A granular delay / cloud processor. Incoming audio is captured into a ring buffer and resprayed as a swarm of overlapping Hann-windowed grains, each read from a delayed, spray-randomised position at a chosen pitch. Controls: Size, Density, Pitch, Spray, Feedback and Mix.

name: Air Exciter
explanation: A psychoacoustic harmonic exciter that adds air and presence. It splits off the upper band, runs it through an asymmetric soft-saturator to synthesise new high-frequency harmonics that were not in the dry signal, brightens that stream with an Air tilt, and blends a controlled amount back on top of the untouched dry path. Controls: Tune, Amount, Air and Mix.

name: Wave Folder
explanation: A west-coast style wavefolder: it drives the signal hard so its peaks fold back on themselves through reflective triangle and sine folds, spawning dense, metallic harmonics instead of flat clipping. Fold sets how many times the wave folds, Symmetry biases the fold axis for even harmonics, and Tone, Output and Mix shape the result.

name: De-Esser
explanation: Tames harsh sibilance on vocals. A sidechain band-pass isolates the sibilant energy around the Frequency control and drives a fast compressor that ducks only the high band when it crosses Threshold, by up to Amount; the ducked highs recombine with the untouched body so the voice loses the harsh 'sss' without going dull. Mix blends against the dry signal.

name: Noise Gate
explanation: A clean noise gate and downward expander. Signal below Threshold is attenuated toward the Range floor while louder material passes untouched; Attack and Release shape the open/close fades, and a Hold time plus built-in hysteresis stop the gate chattering on decays and sustains.

name: Crossover
explanation: A phase-coherent 3-band splitter. Two 4th-order Linkwitz-Riley crossovers divide the signal into low, mid and high bands that sum back flat at unity gain; per-band gain trims let you re-balance the spectrum like a 3-way mixer. Controls: Low Freq, High Freq, Low Gain, Mid Gain, High Gain.

name: Vowel Filter
explanation: A formant filter that gives any source a singing, talkbox colour. Three parallel band-pass resonators are tuned to the first three vocal formants, and a Vowel control morphs the bank smoothly through A, E, I, O and U; Resonance sharpens the peaks and an optional LFO auto-sweeps the vowel for a talking-wah motion.

name: State Filter
explanation: A 2-pole multimode state-variable filter built on a two-integrator core that yields low-pass, high-pass, band-pass and notch outputs at once; a Mode selector taps which one is heard. Resonance climbs all the way to self-oscillation while a soft saturator inside the loop keeps it bounded, and a gentle input Drive adds warmth. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Mode, Drive and dry/wet Mix.

name: Tilt EQ
explanation: A mastering-grade spectral balance shelf. One Tilt control rotates the whole spectrum around an adjustable Pivot frequency — lifting the highs while pulling down the lows (or the reverse) like tipping a see-saw — with a complementary gentle Bass and Treble shelf pair and an output trim.

name: Vocal Wah
explanation: A resonant band-pass wah whose peak is swept either by a manual Pedal treadle or by an envelope follower in Auto mode. Q sharpens the vowel-like peak and Range sets how far the sweep travels, with a dry/wet Mix.

name: Brickwall
explanation: A look-ahead brickwall limiter: a short look-ahead delay feeds a fast peak detector that pulls the gain down before each transient arrives, so the output never exceeds the Ceiling. Threshold drives the input into the limiter, Release sets how quickly the gain recovers, and Gain is a post makeup trim. Transparent on quiet input, an absolute wall on loud.

name: Octave Up
explanation: An octave-up fuzz: a silicon fuzz stage feeds a full-wave rectifier so the signal's negative half folds upward, ringing out a strong upper octave on top of the fuzz. The doubling is strongest on clean single notes. Controls: Fuzz, Octave, Tone and Volume.

name: Dimensional
explanation: A dimensional BBD-style stereo chorus. Two gently pitch-modulated short delay lines per channel are cross-fed left-to-right to bloom a mono source into a wide, animated stereo image with very little obvious wobble. Mode picks four intensity presets; Depth, Width and Mix shape the spread.

name: Twin Phaser
explanation: A dual phaser built from two independent 4-stage allpass engines, each swept by its own LFO. Run them summed for wide, beating notch patterns or chained in series for deep stacked sweeps, with per-engine feedback that sharpens the moving notches into vocal resonances.

name: Magnetic Echo
explanation: An original multi-head magnetic-drum echo. A single circulating loop is read by four fixed playback heads, and a Head Mode selects which head combination sounds for distinct rhythmic multi-tap patterns. Recirculating Swell feedback stacks warm, bandwidth-limited repeats with subtle mechanical flutter. Controls: Time, Swell, Heads, Tone and dry/wet Mix.

name: Pearl Reverb
explanation: A bright early-digital reverb: a dense feedback-delay network of combs diffused through cascaded allpass sections, with a subtly grainy, sparkly converter character. A Program selector reshapes the tail into a smooth Reverb, a longer wide Space, or a nonlinear Gate. Controls: Mix, Decay, Tone, Pre-Delay and Program.

name: Ring Mod
explanation: A classic ring modulator: it multiplies the input by an internal carrier oscillator to generate inharmonic sum and difference sidebands for metallic, clangorous, bell-like tones. The carrier is a sine that blends into a square, and a built-in LFO can sweep its frequency. Controls: Frequency, Waveform, LFO Rate, LFO Depth and dry/wet Mix.

name: Mythic Drive
explanation: A transparent, low-end-preserving overdrive. A clean boosted core is summed with a treble-voiced, soft-clipped gain path that is high-passed before clipping so the bass stays full and clear; an active Treble/Tone control keeps the drive bright and present. Controls: Gain, Treble, Output and clean/driven Mix.

name: Console EQ
explanation: A class-A console channel equaliser: a musical low shelf, a sweepable mid bell with proportional-Q that narrows as you push it, and a high shelf with air. The full strip runs through gentle transformer-style saturation for analog weight. Controls: Low Gain, Mid Freq, Mid Gain, High Gain and Drive.

name: Punch Comp
explanation: A punchy VCA-style dynamics compressor. A stereo-linked RMS+peak detector feeds a dB-domain gain computer with Threshold, Ratio and an over-easy soft knee; Attack and Release shape the gain-reduction envelope and a makeup Gain restores level. It tames loud transients while leaving quiet passages untouched for a clean, controlled, punchy sound.

name: Bias Tremolo
explanation: An amp-style amplitude tremolo: a low-frequency oscillator pulses the signal level for that vintage combo-amp throb. Shape morphs the modulation from a glassy sine sweep into a hard, choppy bias chop, Stereo spreads the L/R phase for a wider image, and Output trims the level. Controls: Rate, Depth, Shape, Stereo and Output.

name: Vibe Phaser
explanation: A photocell vibe and phaser: a four-stage all-pass network is swept by a slow, asymmetric optical LFO (a lamp seen through a light-dependent resistor — fast warm-up, slow cool-down) so the notches glide unevenly for a watery, throbbing movement. Switch between Chorus (phase-shifted path blended with the dry signal) and Vibrato (100% wet pitch wobble), and shape it with Rate, Depth, Intensity and Mix.

name: Ping Delay
explanation: A stereo ping-pong / multi-tap delay. The wet signal cross-feeds between the left and right channels so every repeat bounces to the opposite side, while a chain of extra rhythmic taps thickens the pattern. A tone low-pass darkens each repeat, a width control spreads the bounce, and a saturated feedback path keeps near-max feedback ringing but bounded. Controls: Time, Feedback, Taps, Tone, Width and dry/wet Mix.

name: Space Tape Echo
explanation: A multi-head tape echo: one circulating tape loop is read by three virtual playback heads at fixed taps, and a Mode control selects which head combination is active for different rhythmic repeat patterns. The repeats pass through tape-style HF loss and gentle saturation, a wow/flutter LFO adds vintage pitch wander, and a short ambient tail sits underneath. Controls: Time, Feedback, Mode, Wow, Tone and dry/wet Mix.

name: Pulse Drums
explanation: A hybrid analog + sample drum machine. The body voices (kick, snare, tom) are fully synthesized analog-style, while the metallic voices (closed hat, open hat and crash) are real one-shot recordings baked in from public-domain / CC0 samples and decoded at startup. Each incoming note triggers a voice, and the controls shape tuning, the body decays, snare snap, hat and cymbal decay, brightness, accent and the kick click.

name: Classic Beats
explanation: A four-voice sample-based drum machine in the spirit of the early-'80s sampled rhythm boxes. Real kick, snare, closed hi-hat and clap one-shots — all CC0 / public-domain recordings — are baked into the plugin and triggered polyphonically, with each incoming MIDI note mapped to a voice (note number modulo four) so every key plays a hit. Per-voice levels plus Tone, Decay and Drive shape the groove.

name: Grit Sampler
explanation: A gritty 12-bit drum sampler built around a short public-domain (CC0 / US Navy) drum-cadence segment baked right into the plugin. Each played note pitch-tracks the sample, then deliberately degrades it with variable bit-depth quantization and sample-rate decimation for a crunchy vintage character, finished with a soft-clip drive, a tone low-pass and an amp envelope. Controls: Pitch, Grit, Crush, Tone, Drive, Decay and Level.

name: Tape Choir
explanation: A tape-replay keyboard: each key triggers a sustained, sampled string-ensemble voice that is pitch-tracked, looped with a crossfade for endless sustain, and shaped by an ADSR, tape wow/flutter, a tone low-pass and gentle tape saturation. The embedded voice is a public-domain (CC0) recording, baked into the plugin so it is fully self-contained. Controls: Attack, Release, Tone, Flutter, Drive, Tune and Level.

name: Voltage Drums
explanation: A fully-synthesized analog drum machine. Seven one-shot voices are generated purely by oscillators and filtered noise — kick (pitch-swept sine with a click), snare (tuned sines plus band-passed noise), closed and open hats (a metallic square-oscillator cluster through a high band-pass), clap, tom and cowbell. Each incoming note triggers a voice, and the controls shape tuning, decays, snap, tone and accent.

name: Patch Mono
explanation: An original semi-modular monophonic synth voice: two slightly detuned oscillators (saw plus a triangle-flavoured pulse, with a ring-mod blend in the middle) feed an aggressive two-stage resonant filter — a state-variable high-pass into a Sallen-Key-style low-pass that can self-oscillate and scream. An ADSR drives the amplitude and (by EnvAmount) the low-pass cutoff, with a touch of pre-filter drive. Last-note priority.

name: Reel Saturator
explanation: A tape-style saturation and warmth processor. Drive feeds a tanh soft-saturation (oversampled to tame aliasing) that adds even and odd harmonics with gentle program-dependent compression; Warmth rolls off the highs, Bump adds a resonant low head-bump, and a very subtle wow modulation gives tape motion. Controls: Drive, Warmth, Bump, Mix and Output.

name: Bit Crusher
explanation: A lo-fi digital degrader that quantizes the signal to a variable bit depth and decimates it with a sample-and-hold to a lower effective sample rate, producing grit and aliasing. Controls: Bits, Downsample, Mix and Level.

name: Germanium Fuzz
explanation: A vintage two-transistor germanium fuzz: an AC-coupled, very high-gain stage feeds an asymmetric clipper (soft knee into a hard ceiling) that mimics a cascaded transistor pair. The Bias control shifts the operating point and, turned down, starves the circuit for gated, spitty fuzz; Tone tilts dark-to-bright and Volume sets the output. Controls: Fuzz, Bias, Tone, Volume.

name: Jet Flanger
explanation: A modulated short-delay flanger: a swept fractional delay (about 0.2 to 10 ms) is read with linear interpolation and summed with the dry signal to form a moving comb filter. A bipolar Regen (feedback) control deepens the resonant notches, with negative settings giving the hollow, metallic through-zero-style jet sweep. Controls: Rate, Depth, Regen, Manual base delay and dry/wet Mix.

name: Sweep Phaser
explanation: A 4-stage analog-style phaser: four cascaded all-pass sections are swept by a sine LFO to create two moving notches, with a feedback path for sharper, more resonant sweeps. Controls: Rate, Depth, Feedback and dry/wet Mix.

name: Open Room
explanation: A bright, efficient algorithmic room/hall reverb. Eight parallel damped comb filters per channel feed four series all-pass diffusers, with a small stereo offset between the left/right comb tunings for a wide image. Controls: Mix, Room Size, Damping, Width and Pre-Delay; Mix=0 is bit-exact dry.

name: FM Tines
explanation: A bright, polyphonic FM electric-piano synth. A two-operator phase-modulation engine drives a fast-decaying modulation-index envelope for the classic struck-tine bell attack that settles into a pure sine body, with attack/release amp shaping and a brightness tilt. Controls: Ratio, FMAmount, ModDecay, Attack, Release, Brightness and Level.

name: Analog Poly
explanation: A warm polyphonic analog-style synthesizer with up to eight independent voices. Each voice runs two detuned oscillators (a band-limited saw and pulse) into a resonant 4-pole low-pass shaped by its own filter envelope, then an amplitude ADSR; chords ring with independent contours. Controls: Detune, Cutoff, Resonance, Filter Env Amount and a full ADSR.

name: Acid Bass
explanation: A monophonic squelch-bass synth: one saw<->square morphing oscillator feeding a 4-pole resonant low-pass with a fast-decaying filter envelope for the classic acid sweep. Accent boosts the filter envelope and level on hard hits, Glide slides pitch between notes (last-note priority), and high Resonance pushes toward self-oscillation while staying bounded.

name: Fat Mono
explanation: A fat monophonic analog-style synthesizer: three detuned oscillators (two saws + a pulse) summed thick and driven through a 4-pole resonant transistor-ladder low-pass with tanh saturation in the feedback loop. A dedicated filter envelope sweeps the cutoff on every note, with a separate amplitude envelope plus portamento glide for expressive, last-note-priority lead and bass tones.

name: Steel Plate
explanation: A bright, dense algorithmic plate reverb built from a figure-8 tank: cascaded input-diffusion all-passes feed two cross-coupled, HF-damped delay loops tapped at several internal points for a smooth, fast-building stereo tail. Controls: Mix, Decay, Damping, Pre-Delay and Modulation.

name: Spring Verb
explanation: An original model of a vintage spring-tank reverb. A long cascade of dispersive all-pass delays smears transients into the characteristic chirpy, twangy 'boing', wrapped in a damped feedback loop with two short comb reflections for body. Controls: Decay, Tension (dispersion/brightness), Drip (chirp amount) and dry/wet Mix.

name: Rotary Cabinet
explanation: A twin-rotor rotating-speaker simulator. The signal is split into a low (woofer) and high (horn) band and each is spun through its own virtual driver, producing amplitude tremolo plus a short Doppler pitch vibrato; horn and drum turn at different rates and a Speed control morphs slow chorale to fast tremolo with inertia, so the rotors ramp rather than jump. Two virtual mics build the swirling stereo image.

name: Bucket Echo
explanation: A warm analog bucket-brigade-style delay. A modulated, interpolated delay line feeds a bounded feedback path whose repeats grow progressively darker through an in-loop low-pass, while a slow wow/flutter LFO adds vintage pitch wander. Controls: Time, Feedback, Tone, Modulation and dry/wet Mix.

name: Lush Chorus
explanation: A lush bucket-brigade-style stereo chorus. Short modulated delay lines are read with interpolation and driven by two LFOs in quadrature for a wide image; an Intensity control sweeps from a subtle shimmer to a deep, fast ensemble by scaling rate and depth together, while a dark wet-path low-pass mimics the soft tone of an analogue delay line. Controls: Rate, Depth, Intensity, Mix and Width.

name: Ladder Filter
explanation: A 4-pole (24 dB/oct) resonant transistor-ladder low-pass filter. Four cascaded one-pole stages share a resonance feedback path that can climb all the way to self-oscillation, while gentle tanh saturation in the loop tames the peak and adds warm analog growl. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Drive and dry/wet Mix.

name: Crunch Distortion
explanation: A bright transistor-boost distortion with aggressive midrange and asymmetric op-amp clipping for even-harmonic grit. The active Tone control tilts the spectrum, cutting lows while boosting highs (and vice-versa). Controls: Dist, Tone, Level and dry/wet Mix.

name: Grit Distortion
explanation: Hard-edged, high-gain diode distortion. A high-gain input stage drives an asymmetric hard/diode clipper for an aggressive, cutting character; the signature Filter control is a reversed tone low-pass that gets DARKER as you turn it up. Controls: Distortion, Filter, Volume and dry/wet Mix.

name: Mammoth Fuzz
explanation: A thick, sustaining fuzz. Two cascaded soft-clipping gain stages with inter-stage high-pass filtering deliver long sustain and rich harmonics, while a mid-scooped Tone control tilts the voice between dark/bassy and bright/cutting. Controls: Sustain, Tone, Volume and dry/wet Mix.

name: Velvet EQ
explanation: A passive program equalizer with a vintage tube-EQ character. The low band can boost and attenuate at once — the cut sits just above the boost to carve the famous resonant low end — while the high band adds a broad presence/air peak with a selectable centre plus an independent gentle high-shelf attenuation. Controls: Low Freq, Low Boost, Low Atten, High Boost Freq, High Boost and High Atten.

name: Opto Glow
explanation: A gentle optical leveling amplifier modelled on a classic photocell compressor. A program-driven light source illuminates a resistive photocell whose lag gives a slow, program-dependent attack and a characteristic two-stage release (a quick recovery plus a long, smooth tail); a frequency-aware sidechain and soft knee keep it musical and transparent without pumping. Controls: Peak Reduction, Gain (makeup), a Compress/Limit emphasis, and dry/wet Mix.

name: FET Squeeze
explanation: A fast FET-style peak compressor. A very fast peak detector feeds a dB-domain gain computer with a selectable ratio (about 2:1 to 20:1) and a soft knee, plus a program-dependent release for punchy, glued dynamics. Controls: Input (how hard it hits the detector), Attack, Release, Ratio and Output makeup gain.

name: Emerald Overdrive
explanation: Smooth, mid-focused tube-style overdrive. A band-limited gain stage feeds a symmetric soft-clipper (op-amp + anti-parallel diode model); the low end is cleaned before clipping for the signature mid-hump, then a post tone low-pass and output level shape the result. Controls: Drive, Tone, Level and dry/wet Mix.

name: Shimmer Hall
explanation: Bright, modulated digital hall reverb. An 8-line feedback delay network whose read taps are modulated by per-line LFOs (interpolated fractional delays) to dissolve metallic ringing into a lush, chorused tail. A Tone control trades a dark, damped decay for open high-frequency air. Controls: Mix, Size, Decay, Tone, Modulation depth and Pre-Delay.

name: Vast Hall
explanation: Large digital hall reverb. An input diffusion chain feeds an 8-line feedback delay network with a lossless Householder feedback matrix and per-line high-frequency damping, for a smooth, dense, long-tailed hall. Controls: dry/wet Mix, room Size, Decay (RT60 up to ~12 s), HF Damping, Pre-Delay and stereo Width.

name: VibeSynth
explanation: A monophonic analog-style synthesizer: three anti-aliased oscillators with per-osc octave and detune, a noise source, a four-pole resonant ladder filter with drive, independent filter and amplifier ADSR envelopes, and portamento glide. A stereo effects rack adds chorus, ping-pong delay and reverb, with live oscilloscope + spectrum displays. Play it with the keyboard, your computer keys (a–k), or MIDI.

name: Warm Lowpass
explanation: A gentle one-pole low-pass with output gain. Feed it one of the royalty-free samples, a test tone, your mic, or your own file to hear it work.

name: Saw Lead
explanation: A monophonic sawtooth lead with a soft AR envelope. Play it with the on-screen keyboard, your computer keys (a–k), or a MIDI controller.
